prompt: Find the verse passage that names a former 'abode of Caesar' as an 'eternal symbol of the Ummah's power and grandeur', and that hears 'a voice arises' from the tomb of Ayyub Ansari proclaiming the city the very heart of the Islamic nation. Identify the work and chapter and quote the relevant lines verbatim.
rationale: The pairing of Constantinople (called 'the abode of Caesar') with the tomb of Ayyub Ansari speaking from within it is unique to the chapter Bilad-e-Islamiya in Bang-e-Dara Part 3. Part 2's Saqaliya laments Sicily/Baghdad/Granada but never names Constantinople or Ayyub Ansari; Part 1 has no comparable pan-Islamic city catalogue.

prompt: Locate the long verse complaint addressed to God that, in a single rhetorical stretch, asks who tore out 'Khyber's mighty door', who 'laid Caesar's city flat upon the floor', who smashed the man-made idols, and who 'extinguished Zoroaster's sacred fire'. Identify the work and chapter and quote the stanza verbatim.
rationale: This four-question catalog (Khyber's door + Caesar's city + Zoroaster's sacred fire extinguished, all in one breath) is uniquely from the chapter Shikwa in Bang-e-Dara Part 3 — a grievance addressed directly to God. Parts 1 and 2 contain neither this fixed enumeration nor the rhetorical mode of complaint to God.

prompt: Find the short anthem-like verse that opens 'From Chin to Arab lands, our story flows' and proceeds to apostrophise both 'O Garden of Andalusia' (asking whether it remembers when 'on your branches high our nest in safety lay') and 'O wave of Tigris' (saying the river itself tells the speaker's story). Identify the work and chapter and quote those two apostrophes verbatim.
rationale: The exact paired direct address to the Garden of Andalusia and to the wave of the Tigris, framed by the 'Chin-to-Arab' opening, is uniquely from Tarana-e-Milli in Bang-e-Dara Part 3. Part 2's Saqaliya names Granada and Baghdad in third person but never apostrophises Andalusia or the Tigris; Part 1 has no such pan-Islamic millat anthem.
